glossary.def: Turning a categorical column of K distinct levels into K binary indicator columns, exactly one of which is 1 per row. It implies no ordering between categories but widens the feature matrix linearly with cardinality.

Mapping ordered categories to ascending integers (small=0, medium=1, large=2). Correct only when the categories are genuinely ranked; applied to nominal data it invents a false order the model will exploit.

Replacing each category with the mean of the target for that category, usually smoothed toward the global mean. It compresses high-cardinality columns to one informative feature but leaks the label unless fit out-of-fold.

An encoding for binary targets that replaces each category with the log-ratio of its share of positives to its share of negatives. It lives on the log-odds scale and underpins the Information Value used in credit scoring.
Computing a target-dependent encoding for a row using only the other cross-validation folds, so no row ever contributes to its own encoded value. The standard defence against target-encoding leakage.

Subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation so a feature has mean 0 and unit variance. The default scaler for linear models, SVMs, PCA and neural nets; not robust to outliers and does not bound the range.

Centering by the median and scaling by the interquartile range instead of the mean and standard deviation, so outliers in the tails barely affect the transform. Preferred when the data has fat tails or known errors.

A one-parameter family of power transforms, (x^lambda - 1)/lambda (log at lambda=0), applied to strictly positive data with lambda chosen by maximum likelihood to make the distribution as Gaussian as possible. Yeo-Johnson extends it to zero and negative values.

refs.cite: Micci-Barreca, D. (2001). A Preprocessing Scheme for High-Cardinality Categorical Attributes in Classification and Prediction Problems. ACM SIGKDD Explorations 3(1).
Box, G. E. P. & Cox, D. R. (1964). An Analysis of Transformations. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society B 26(2).
Yeo, I.-K. & Johnson, R. A. (2000). A New Family of Power Transformations to Improve Normality or Symmetry. Biometrika 87(4).
Kuhn, M. & Johnson, K. (2019). Feature Engineering and Selection: A Practical Approach for Predictive Models. CRC Press.
Pedregosa, F. et al. (2011). Scikit-learn: Machine Learning in Python. JMLR 12.
Liu, H., Hussain, F., Tan, C. L. & Dash, M. (2002). Discretization: An Enabling Technique. Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery 6.
